---
title: "residualtest"
output: html_document
date: "2026-05-28"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots
# Standardised residuals and the residual variance estimate

This note drafts the residual-scaling logic for inspection.

## Factual decision logic

- The model errors \(\varepsilon_i\) are unobservable; the raw residuals
  \(e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i\) are their observable counterparts.
- A global residual standard error estimates the unobservable \(\sigma\), but
  the variance of each raw residual also depends on its leverage.
- Standardised residuals divide each raw residual by an estimate of its own
  residual standard deviation.

## Raw residuals

After fitting the corresponding linear model, the raw residual for observation
\(i\) is

\begin{equation}
e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i,
(\#eq:raw-residual)
\end{equation}

where \(y_i\) is the observed response and \(\hat{y}_i\) is the fitted value.

The residual standard error estimates the unobservable model error standard
deviation \(\sigma\):

\begin{equation}
SE_\text{res} =
\sqrt{\frac{\sum_{r=1}^{N} e_r^2}{N-k}},
(\#eq:residual-standard-error)
\end{equation}

where \(N\) is the total sample size and \(k\) is the number of fitted model
parameters.

## Standardised residuals

The raw residuals are not all equally variable. For observation \(i\),

\begin{equation}
\operatorname{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2(1-h_i),
(\#eq:residual-variance-leverage)
\end{equation}

where \(h_i\) is the leverage of observation \(i\). Thus, using only
\(SE_\text{res}\) standardises all residuals by the same global estimate of
\(\sigma\), but does not account for the observation-specific leverage term.

The internally standardised residual is therefore

\begin{equation}
r_i =
\frac{e_i}{SE_\text{res}\sqrt{1-h_i}}.
(\#eq:standardised-residual)
\end{equation}

This is the residual scale returned by `rstandard()` for a fitted `lm` object.

## Leverage in simple linear regression

For simple linear regression with one numeric predictor, the leverage has the
closed form

\begin{equation}
h_i = \frac{1}{N} +
      \frac{(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{\sum_{r=1}^{N}(x_r-\bar{x})^2}.
(\#eq:leverage-simple-regression)
\end{equation}

Here \(x_i\) is the predictor value of observation \(i\), and \(\bar{x}\) is
the predictor mean.

## Cook's distance on the standardised-residual scale

With \(r_i\) defined in Eq. \@ref(eq:standardised-residual), Cook's distance
can be written as

\begin{equation}
D_i =
\frac{r_i^2 h_i}{k(1-h_i)}.
(\#eq:cooks-distance-standardised)
\end{equation}

For simple linear regression, \(k = 2\). This form combines the size of the
standardised residual with the leverage of the observation.
You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
